claims.statement: The official leaderboard page selects performances_generation.json as the current release_v6 data artifact.
The result artifact identifies an Aider version, edit format, command, and model, so EvalRank treats each row as an agent system configuration rather than a direct-prompt model result.
The official website publishes the display leaderboard while the Apache-2.0 repository pins the 89-task methodology and current merged submissions.
On 2026-07-15, EvalRank's read-only source comparison found every published row matched one current repository submission and repository-only submissions represented unpublished history.
The pinned official website and Harbor clients document anonymous leaderboard-read access for the public Terminal-Bench 2.1 board.
The pinned repository migrations define the current-only boundary: judge_url was removed when hack metrics were introduced, and the later migration introduced signed hack labels.
The current-schema adapter joins the anonymous leaderboard response to an exact repository commit, excludes repository-only history from observations, and covers no trajectories.
LiveSWEBench separates autonomous agentic programming, targeted editing, and autocompletion instead of publishing one undifferentiated coding construct.
The official leaderboard script resolves data_overall.csv; the previously assumed raw Git path is not the current data authority.
EvalRank treats top-cohort compression as a hypothesis to measure on a dated AndroidWorld leaderboard snapshot, not as an established saturation fact.
FACTS Parametric is the suite component that measures factual knowledge from model parameters without search or other external tools.
MMLU-Pro is built around questions adapted from original MMLU, and MMLU-ProX carries the resulting questions across translated language versions.
Global-MMLU translates and professionally or communally reviews original MMLU questions across languages.
MMLU-ProX is built on MMLU-Pro and uses identical translated questions to enable direct cross-linguistic comparison.
EvalRank treats top-cohort compression as a hypothesis to test on a dated MMMU-Pro result snapshot, not as a source-established saturation fact.
The Apache-2.0 SRE leaderboard reports multiple-trial incident diagnosis and repair outcomes with native uncertainty fields.

ITBench-AA is an independent implementation and rerun layer over ITBench SRE tasks, combining 40 public tasks with 19 private tasks in one reported result, so it remains a feed of the ITBench family rather than independent evidence.
The official ITBench repository licenses its harness code under Apache-2.0.
The official repository's Apache-2.0 license covers its published SRE scenarios and task definitions.
Apache-2.0 grants use and distribution rights without a noncommercial restriction.
Apache-2.0 permits redistribution of the licensed work subject to its notice conditions, so independently produced result records may be redistributed.
The official repository publishes its Kubernetes environment definitions under Apache-2.0.
Apache-2.0 grants reproduction rights for the repository's licensed benchmark artifacts, permitting retained copies subject to license conditions.
Apache-2.0 grants public display and distribution rights for licensed and derivative material, permitting publication of independently derived scores.
The Apache-2.0 SRE leaderboard reports multiple-trial incident diagnosis and repair outcomes with native uncertainty fields.

SREGym imports ITBench and AIOpsLab problems, so its aggregate cannot count as independent evidence; only a hash-deduplicated new-task stratum could qualify separately.
Build, configuration, monitoring, testing, and delivery-pipeline tasks form a DevOps lifecycle construct and must not be pooled with live SRE incident repair.
The official application identifies 2026-06-25 as the current release and fetches its table and category artifacts as one pair.

benchmark_family.quarantine_reason: Task design and contamination concerns require repaired task/configuration evidence and replay.

feed.quarantine_reason: Task design and contamination concerns require repaired task/configuration evidence and replay.

claims.statement: The official audit reports task-design and contamination defects that prevent the unrepaired benchmark from supporting current system comparisons.

benchmark_family.quarantine_reason: Documented broken-task rates require a repaired task set and replay before admission.

feed.quarantine_reason: Documented broken-task rates require a repaired task set and replay before admission.

claims.statement: The official audit documents broken tasks at a rate that requires a repaired task set and replay before admission.

benchmark_family.quarantine_reason: Current rows erase system kind and use competitor count as task count; exact identity and uncertainty must be repaired.

feed.quarantine_reason: Current rows erase system kind and use competitor count as task count; exact identity and uncertainty must be repaired.

claims.statement: EvalRank's code audit found that the current composite rows do not retain exact system kind and use competitor count as task count, so identity and uncertainty are not admission-ready.
GDPval v1 covers 1,320 professional tasks across 44 occupations in nine U.S. industries, with a 220-task open gold subset.
The checked public release identifies the benchmark as the first version and describes future interactive versions as planned work.
The reported aggregate spans 44 occupations selected from nine industries rather than one homogeneous occupation.
GDPval v1 supplies prompts, reference files, and context in one-shot tasks and does not measure iterative context building or revision.
Because tasks require heterogeneous document, slide, spreadsheet, diagram, and multimedia deliverables, EvalRank will compare only explicitly identified tool and scaffold configurations.
The primary evaluation uses blinded occupational experts to compare and rank AI and human deliverables.
The experimental automated grader estimates expert preferences and is explicitly described as not reliable enough to replace expert grading.
MLE-bench v1 evaluates agents on 75 Kaggle machine-learning competitions; its low-complexity lite split contains 22 competitions.
As checked on 2026-07-09, the repository says v1 fixes are deferred to an upcoming v2 release and new leaderboard submissions have been paused since 2026-04-24.
The official repository states that new leaderboard submissions have been paused since 2026-04-24 while a fairer, more comparable process is developed.
The official Known Issues list records preparation, grading, checksum, leakage, missing-data, and crowded-leaderboard defects in v1 competitions.
The maintainers say batched fixes and version-separated results are planned for an upcoming v2 release.
The canonical full protocol recommends 24 hours, 36 vCPUs, 440 GB RAM, one 24 GB A10 GPU, and at least three seeds across 75 competitions.
Because the official protocol says resource changes can degrade results and evaluates agent systems rather than bare models, EvalRank will not pool unlike scaffolds or resource envelopes.
The benchmark is constructed from 75 Kaggle competitions and uses their data and leaderboard-derived medal thresholds.
The paper studies pre-training familiarity, while the official Known Issues list identifies public labels and test-label leakage in multiple competitions.
PaperBench evaluates end-to-end replication of 20 ICML 2024 Spotlight and Oral machine-learning papers.
The checked canonical release is the 2025-04-02 PaperBench task set and its maintained frontier-evals implementation.
All 20 source papers are ICML 2024 Spotlight or Oral machine-learning papers.
Agents must understand each paper, develop a codebase, and execute experiments to replicate it from scratch.
Executed submissions are graded against hierarchical rubrics by an LLM-based judge whose performance is separately evaluated.
The official setup requires separately hydrated Git LFS data, and some papers require external OpenAI or Hugging Face access or non-redistributable JudgeEval assets.
The benchmark uses separate rollout, GPU reproduction, and grading stages, with official leaderboard runs reporting 24-hour and 36-hour agent limits.
CORE-Bench v1.1 contains 39 corrected mainline computational-reproducibility tasks, while CORE-Bench OOD contains 19 field-shift tasks.
The June 2026 primary paper introduces CORE-Bench v1.1 and CORE-Bench OOD and treats both as active benchmarks that may be updated as validity threats are found.
The paper reports that the top five agents are statistically indistinguishable on accuracy for both v1.1 and OOD.
v1.1 corrects task-level errors and exploitable shortcuts, and the authors plan continued updates when new validity threats surface.
The paper reports material model-scaffold interaction, including an approximately 44-point difference for the same GPT-5.4 setting across two scaffolds.

Because tasks depend on separately owned Code Ocean capsules and the primary sources do not establish one blanket redistribution grant, EvalRank requires a per-capsule rights audit before retention or publication.
The v1.1 study finds retained signal in efficiency, reliability, model-versus-scaffold behavior, out-of-distribution generalization, and human-agent uplift despite accuracy compression.
